---
title: "Coasting Speed Function Explained (with OpenStax University Physics 1 references)"
format: gfm
---

## Overview

This document explains the function below using ideas from **OpenStax _University Physics Volume 1_**:

- [Ch. 5: Newton's Laws of Motion](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-introduction)
- [Sec. 6.2: Friction](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-2-friction)
- [Sec. 6.4: Drag Force and Terminal Speed](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed)
- [Sec. 7.2: Kinetic Energy](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-2-kinetic-energy)
- [Sec. 7.3: Work-Energy Theorem](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-3-work-energy-theorem)
- [Sec. 8.1: Potential Energy of a System](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/8-1-potential-energy-of-a-system)

Note: earlier drafts referenced "Ch. 4 Newton's Laws." In this OpenStax edition, Newton's laws are covered in **Chapter 5**.

The function models a cyclist **coasting** (no pedaling) across route segments, where each segment has a grade and a length. It compounds the speed: the end speed of one segment becomes the start speed of the next.

---

## The function being explained

```python
import numpy as np

def compute_coast_speed(df, v0=0.0,
                        m=85.0,        # kg (rider + bike)
                        CdA=0.40,      # m^2
                        Crr=0.005,     # -
                        rho=1.225,     # kg/m^3
                        g=9.81):
    """
    Coasting speed compounded across segments.
    Assumes: downhill segments have NEGATIVE grade (e.g., -0.10 for -10%).
    Requires columns:
      - step_grade (decimal)
      - step_dist_m (meters)
    """

    grade = df["step_grade"].fillna(0).to_numpy(dtype=float)
    L = df["step_dist_m"].fillna(0).to_numpy(dtype=float)

    speeds = np.empty(len(df), dtype=float)
    v = float(v0)

    k_drag = rho * CdA / m  # constant in the v^2 drag term

    for i in range(len(df)):
        # Convert "negative = downhill" into a positive downhill component
        downhill_component = -grade[i]  # e.g. grade=-0.10 => downhill_component=+0.10

        # Update v^2 using a simple energy/force approximation over distance
        v2 = (
            v*v
            + 2.0 * g * (downhill_component - Crr) * L[i]
            - k_drag * v*v * L[i]
        )

        v = np.sqrt(v2) if v2 > 0 else 0.0
        speeds[i] = v

    df = df.copy()
    df["coast_speed_mps"] = speeds
    df["coast_speed_mph"] = speeds * 2.2369362920544
    return df
```

---

## 1) Gravity on an incline

**OpenStax references:**
- [Ch. 5 introduction to forces and Newton's laws](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-introduction)
- [Sec. 5.3 Newton's Second Law](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-3-newtons-second-law)
- [Sec. 6.2 Friction](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-2-friction)

On an incline, the component of gravity **along the slope** is:

$$
F_{\parallel} = mg\sin\theta
$$

For small road angles, a **grade** (rise/run) is well-approximated by:

$$
\sin\theta \approx \text{grade}
$$

This code stores downhill as a **negative grade**, so it flips the sign:

```python
downhill_component = -grade[i]
```

so that a downhill becomes positive (e.g., `-0.10 -> +0.10`). This makes the downhill acceleration term naturally positive.

---

## 2) Rolling resistance

**OpenStax reference:**
- [Sec. 6.2 Friction](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-2-friction)

Rolling resistance is modeled like a frictional force proportional to the normal force:

$$
F_{rr} = C_{rr}N
$$

For gentle slopes, the normal force is approximately:

$$
N \approx mg
$$

so:

$$
F_{rr} \approx C_{rr}mg
$$

and the associated acceleration magnitude is:

$$
a_{rr} = \frac{F_{rr}}{m} \approx gC_{rr}
$$

The code combines gravity and rolling resistance inside:

```python
2.0 * g * (downhill_component - Crr) * L[i]
```

---

## 3) Aerodynamic drag (quadratic in speed)

**OpenStax reference:**
- [Sec. 6.4 Drag Force and Terminal Speed](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed)

OpenStax gives a common quadratic drag model:

$$
F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2
$$

The function uses `CdA` as the combined factor $C_dA$ and forms a constant:

```python
k_drag = rho * CdA / m
```

This is a compact way to keep track of how drag scales with:

- air density $\rho$
- effective frontal area/drag coefficient $C_dA$
- inverse mass $1/m$

The drag term appears as a subtraction proportional to $v^2$:

```python
- k_drag * v*v * L[i]
```

Because drag grows rapidly with speed, it produces a natural leveling-off of downhill speed.

---

## 4) Why the update is written in terms of $v^2$

**OpenStax references:**
- [Sec. 7.2 Kinetic Energy](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-2-kinetic-energy)
- [Sec. 7.3 Work-Energy Theorem](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-3-work-energy-theorem)

Kinetic energy is:

$$
K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2
$$

The work-energy theorem states:

$$
W_{net} = \Delta K
$$

If we approximate the net force along the direction of motion as constant over a small segment length $L$:

$$
W_{net} \approx F_{net}L
$$

Using $F_{net} = ma$, we get:

$$
maL = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v^2 - v_0^2)
$$

Cancel $m$ and multiply by 2:

$$
v^2 = v_0^2 + 2aL
$$

That is exactly the structure used in the function: it updates $v^2$ across each segment, then takes a square root to get $v$.

---

## 5) Terminal speed appears naturally

**OpenStax reference:**
- [Sec. 6.4 Drag Force and Terminal Speed](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed)

Terminal speed happens when the downhill driving term is balanced by resistive drag (and rolling resistance). In a simplified steady-speed picture:

$$
g(\text{grade} - C_{rr}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\frac{\rho C_dA}{m}v^2
$$

Solving for $v$ gives a terminal-speed estimate:

$$
v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2mg(\text{grade}-C_{rr})}{\rho C_dA}}
$$

In the step-by-step simulation, as $v$ increases, the drag term grows until further acceleration is canceled, so the computed speed tends toward a stable value on sustained downhills.

---

## 6) Continuous-equation interpretation (what the code is discretizing)

**OpenStax references:**
- [Sec. 5.3 Newton's Second Law](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-3-newtons-second-law)
- [Sec. 6.4 Drag Force and Terminal Speed](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed)
- Related kinematics background: [Ch. 3 introduction](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/3-introduction)

A common downhill-with-drag model is:

$$
m\frac{dv}{dt}
=
mg(\text{grade} - C_{rr})
-
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_dA v^2
$$

The code effectively integrates the motion **by distance** in small chunks, using a work/energy-style update over each segment length.

---

## 7) Default parameter values and why they were chosen

These defaults are practical values for a recreational road/gravel rider in near-sea-level conditions. They are not universal constants.

| Parameter | Default | Why this is a reasonable default | When to change it |
|---|---:|---|---|
| `v0` | `0.0 m/s` | Conservative start: assumes each run begins from rest. Useful if route starts from a stop sign, trailhead, or paused state. | Set from measured incoming speed if you are stitching this model into a larger simulation. |
| `m` | `85.0 kg` | Typical combined mass of rider + bike + gear. Mass increases gravitational driving force and inertia together, so speed sensitivity is moderate but real. | Change for lighter/heavier riders, loaded bikes, or e-bikes. |
| `CdA` | `0.40 m^2` | Common order-of-magnitude for an upright-ish cyclist posture. Captures rider position and bike aero profile in one value. | Lower for aggressive aero position, higher for upright riding, loose clothing, or bags. |
| `Crr` | `0.002` | Represents low rolling resistance on smooth pavement with efficient tires. Keeps rolling losses present but smaller than gravity on meaningful descents. | Increase for rough pavement, gravel, soft tires, or wider/off-road tires. |
| `rho` | `1.225 kg/m^3` | Standard sea-level air density used in many textbook drag examples. | Lower at altitude or hotter air; higher in cold dense air. |
| `g` | `9.81 m/s^2` | Standard Earth gravitational acceleration. | Rarely changed unless modeling another planet or using local geodesy precision. |

Short intuition check:

- On shallow downhills, `Crr` can be a large fraction of the gravity component, so speed may not grow much.
- On steeper/longer downhills, drag (`CdA`, `rho`) dominates and sets an effective terminal speed.
- Because `m` is in both driving and drag balance terms, changing mass usually shifts speed less than people expect, while `CdA` often shifts it more.

---

## Chapter mapping summary

| Concept in the code | OpenStax UP1 chapter |
|---|---|
| Free-body diagram and net force along slope | [Ch. 5](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-introduction), [Sec. 5.3](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/5-3-newtons-second-law) |
| Friction / rolling resistance modeling | [Sec. 6.2](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-2-friction) |
| Quadratic drag force | [Sec. 6.4](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed) |
| Terminal speed | [Sec. 6.4](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/6-4-drag-force-and-terminal-speed) |
| Kinetic energy | [Sec. 7.2](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-2-kinetic-energy) |
| Work-Energy Theorem | [Sec. 7.3](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/7-3-work-energy-theorem) |
| Gravitational potential energy context | [Sec. 8.1](https://openstax.org/books/university-physics-volume-1/pages/8-1-potential-energy-of-a-system) |

---

## Big picture

This function is best viewed as:

> A **discrete work-energy** update (Ch. 7)
> using Newtonian forces on an incline (Ch. 5 and Ch. 6)
> with **rolling resistance** (Sec. 6.2) and **quadratic drag** (Sec. 6.4),
> compounded segment-by-segment along a route.
